title: Statistical performance indicators (SPI): Pillar 5 data infrastructure score (scale 0-100)
definition: The data infrastructure  pillar  overall score measures the hard and soft infrastructure segments, itemizing essential cross cutting requirements for an effective statistical system.  The segments are: (i) legislation and governance covering the existence of laws and a functioning institutional framework for the statistical system; (ii) standards and methods addressing compliance with recognized frameworks and concepts; (iii) skills including level of skills within the statistical system and among users (statistical literacy); (iv) partnerships reflecting the need for the statistical system to be inclusive and coherent; and (v) finance mobilized both domestically and from donors.

editorial.shortHook: A candidate map for statistical data infrastructure.
editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Turkiye, Russian Federation, New Zealand, while the lightest include Eritrea, Turkmenistan, Papua New Guinea; that contrast is the fastest way to read the statistical data infrastructure pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Statistical data infrastructure may add useful WORLDPRINT variety once source coverage, unit clarity, map interest, and distractor ambiguity are reviewed.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Undernourishment can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Nurses and midwives can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.

editorial.difficultyReason: Expert difficulty: the map can be mistaken for adjacent indicators unless players probe a high, low, and middle country.
regionalSignals: Per-person development maps are shaped by income, resource rents, and city-state scale.
